  • Hide All Code

What Actually Predicts a Hitter’s Next Season?

A Statcast-era OPS projection, built one regression at a time

Evan Heffelfinger

June 2026

The question

Every offseason, fans and front offices guess what a hitter will do next year. The natural instinct is to look at what he just did. But last season’s line is a mix of skill and luck, and luck doesn’t carry over — which is why “he’ll repeat last year” is a famously bad bet.

This is a forecasting problem with a built-in trap. OPS is defined as on-base plus slugging, so using a season’s own OBP and SLG to “predict” its OPS is circular, it isn’t prediction at all. The honest version: predict a player’s OPS in a season using only what was knowable before it. That’s what this does, and it asks one extra question that the Statcast era makes possible: do a hitter’s “expected” stats (what he should have produced based on how hard and at what angle he hit the ball) forecast next year better than what he actually produced?

pkgs <- c("Lahman", "dplyr", "tidyr", "tibble", "purrr", "readr", "httr",
          "ggplot2", "broom", "scales")
new <- pkgs[!pkgs %in% rownames(installed.packages())]
if (length(new)) install.packages(new)
invisible(lapply(c(pkgs, "baseballr"), library, character.only = TRUE))

set.seed(1)
dir.create("cache", showWarnings = FALSE)

TARGET_START   <- 2016   # Statcast-era target seasons
STATCAST_START <- 2015
TRAIN_END      <- 2022   # train on targets <= this; validate on later years
MIN_PA_TARGET  <- 250; MIN_PA_LAG1 <- 100
col_a <- "#2C7FB8"; col_b <- "#800000"; col_grey <- "#BDBDBD"
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
          theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
                plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")))

The data

Each row is one player-season we want to project (qualified hitters, 2016 onward). The features come only from earlier seasons: a Marcel-style blend of the prior three years’ OPS (recency- and playing-time-weighted, regressed toward league average and age-adjusted), prior-year plate discipline and batted-ball luck, age, home-park factor, and joined in from Baseball Savant via the Chadwick id crosswalk, the prior year’s expected wOBA (xwOBA).

bat_ps <- Batting |>
  filter(yearID >= 2012, yearID != 2020) |>
  group_by(playerID, yearID) |>
  summarise(across(c(AB, H, X2B, X3B, HR, BB, SO, HBP, SH, SF),
                   ~ sum(.x, na.rm = TRUE)), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(PA = AB + BB + HBP + SF, TB = H + X2B + 2*X3B + 3*HR,
         OBP = ifelse((AB+BB+HBP+SF) > 0, (H+BB+HBP)/(AB+BB+HBP+SF), NA_real_),
         SLG = ifelse(AB > 0, TB/AB, NA_real_), OPS = OBP + SLG,
         Kpct = ifelse(PA > 0, SO/PA, NA_real_),
         BABIP = ifelse((AB-SO-HR+SF) > 0, (H-HR)/(AB-SO-HR+SF), NA_real_))

league_year <- bat_ps |> group_by(yearID) |>
  summarise(H=sum(H), AB=sum(AB), BB=sum(BB), HBP=sum(HBP), SF=sum(SF),
            X2B=sum(X2B), X3B=sum(X3B), HR=sum(HR), .groups="drop") |>
  mutate(lg_ops = (H+BB+HBP)/(AB+BB+HBP+SF) + (H+X2B+2*X3B+3*HR)/AB) |>
  select(yearID, lg_ops)
lg_ops_overall <- mean(league_year$lg_ops)

people_df <- tryCatch(get("People"), error = function(e) get("Master"))
bio <- people_df |>
  transmute(playerID, bbrefID, birthYear, birthMonth, bats,
            nameFirst, nameLast)
debut_year <- Batting |> group_by(playerID) |>
  summarise(first_year = min(yearID), .groups = "drop")
park_year <- Batting |>
  filter(yearID >= 2012, yearID != 2020) |>
  group_by(playerID, yearID, teamID) |> summarise(AB=sum(AB), .groups="drop") |>
  group_by(playerID, yearID) |> slice_max(AB, n=1, with_ties=FALSE) |> ungroup() |>
  left_join(select(Teams, yearID, teamID, BPF), by=c("yearID","teamID")) |>
  transmute(playerID, yearID, park_bpf = as.numeric(BPF))

# Lagged + Marcel blend
lag1 <- bat_ps |> transmute(playerID, yearID = yearID + 1,
  lag1_OPS = OPS, lag1_SLG = SLG, lag1_Kpct = Kpct, lag1_BABIP = BABIP, lag1_PA = PA)
lag2 <- bat_ps |> transmute(playerID, yearID = yearID + 2, lag2_OPS = OPS, lag2_PA = PA)
lag3 <- bat_ps |> transmute(playerID, yearID = yearID + 3, lag3_OPS = OPS, lag3_PA = PA)

model_df <- bat_ps |> transmute(playerID, yearID, ops_target = OPS, pa_target = PA) |>
  left_join(lag1, by=c("playerID","yearID")) |>
  left_join(lag2, by=c("playerID","yearID")) |>
  left_join(lag3, by=c("playerID","yearID")) |>
  left_join(bio, by="playerID") |>
  left_join(debut_year, by="playerID") |>
  left_join(mutate(park_year, yearID = yearID + 1), by=c("playerID","yearID")) |>
  left_join(transmute(league_year, yearID = yearID + 1, lag1_lg_ops = lg_ops), by="yearID") |>
  mutate(age = yearID - birthYear - ifelse(!is.na(birthMonth) & birthMonth > 6, 1, 0),
         age_sq = age^2)

w1<-5; w2<-4; w3<-3; REG<-300
model_df <- model_df |>
  mutate(o1=coalesce(lag1_OPS,0), p1=coalesce(lag1_PA,0),
         o2=coalesce(lag2_OPS,0), p2=coalesce(lag2_PA,0),
         o3=coalesce(lag3_OPS,0), p3=coalesce(lag3_PA,0),
         num=w1*p1*o1+w2*p2*o2+w3*p3*o3, den=w1*p1+w2*p2+w3*p3,
         lgb=coalesce(lag1_lg_ops, lg_ops_overall),
         af = ifelse(age<29, 1+(29-age)*0.006, 1+(29-age)*0.003),
         marcel_ops = ((num + lgb*REG)/(den + REG))*af) |>
  select(-o1,-o2,-o3,-p1,-p2,-p3,-num,-den,-lgb,-af) |>
  filter(yearID >= TARGET_START, !is.na(lag1_OPS),
         lag1_PA >= MIN_PA_LAG1, pa_target >= MIN_PA_TARGET,
         !is.na(park_bpf), bats %in% c("L","R","B"),
         !is.na(age), !is.na(lag1_Kpct), !is.na(lag1_BABIP))
# --- Pull prior-season xwOBA from Baseball Savant (cached after first run) ---
cache_get <- function(key, expr) {
  f <- file.path("cache", paste0(key, ".rds"))
  if (file.exists(f)) return(readRDS(f)); val <- force(expr); saveRDS(val, f); val
}
get_savant_csv <- function(url) {
  r <- httr::GET(url, httr::user_agent("Mozilla/5.0")); httr::stop_for_status(r)
  readr::read_csv(httr::content(r, as="text", encoding="UTF-8"), show_col_types = FALSE)
}
fetch_xwoba <- function(y) cache_get(sprintf("xstats_%d", y), {
  u <- sprintf(paste0("https://baseballsavant.mlb.com/leaderboard/expected_",
                      "statistics?type=batter&year=%d&min=50&csv=true"), y)
  ex <- get_savant_csv(u)
  tibble(key_mlbam = as.integer(ex$player_id), yearID = y,
         sc_xwoba = ex$est_woba, sc_xslg = ex$est_slg, sc_woba = ex$woba)
})

chad <- cache_get("chadwick", baseballr::chadwick_player_lu())
pid2mlbam <- bio |> filter(!is.na(bbrefID), bbrefID != "") |>
  distinct(playerID, bbrefID) |>
  inner_join(chad |> filter(!is.na(key_mlbam), !is.na(key_bbref), key_bbref != "") |>
               transmute(bbrefID = key_bbref, key_mlbam = as.integer(key_mlbam)),
             by = "bbrefID") |> distinct(playerID, key_mlbam)

statcast <- purrr::map_dfr(STATCAST_START:max(model_df$yearID), fetch_xwoba) |>
  inner_join(pid2mlbam, by = "key_mlbam")
sc_lag1 <- statcast |> transmute(playerID, yearID = yearID + 1,
  lag1_sc_xwoba = sc_xwoba, lag1_sc_xslg = sc_xslg, lag1_sc_woba = sc_woba)

dat <- model_df |>
  left_join(sc_lag1, by = c("playerID", "yearID")) |>
  filter(!is.na(lag1_sc_xwoba)) |>          # keep rows matched to Statcast
  mutate(bats = factor(bats))

cat(sprintf("Player-seasons used: %d  |  matched to Statcast: %.0f%%  |  years: %s\n",
            nrow(dat), 100 * nrow(dat) / nrow(model_df),
            paste(range(dat$yearID), collapse = "-")))
## Player-seasons used: 2194  |  matched to Statcast: 100%  |  years: 2016-2025

The puzzle: which prior-season number sees the future best?

Before modeling, a simple question — of the things we could read off last year, which one correlates most with next year’s OPS? If xwOBA (a quality-of-contact measure that ignores whether the ball found a glove) keeps pace with raw OPS, that’s the tell that expected stats are worth carrying.

cwt <- function(x) cor(x, dat$ops_target, use = "complete.obs")
cor_df <- tibble(
  metric = c("Last yr OPS (actual)", "Last yr SLG (actual)",
             "Last yr wOBA (actual)", "Last yr xwOBA (expected)",
             "Last yr xSLG (expected)"),
  r = c(cwt(dat$lag1_OPS), cwt(dat$lag1_SLG), cwt(dat$lag1_sc_woba),
        cwt(dat$lag1_sc_xwoba), cwt(dat$lag1_sc_xslg)),
  kind = c("actual","actual","actual","expected","expected"))

ggplot(cor_df, aes(r, reorder(metric, r), fill = kind)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", r)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(actual = col_grey, expected = col_a), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +
  labs(title = "Best single leading indicator of next-season OPS",
       subtitle = "Correlation with next year's OPS (2016+). Blue = Statcast expected stats.",
       x = "Correlation with next-season OPS", y = NULL)

Building the model, one layer at a time

A projection is really a stack of ideas. We add them as regression terms and watch the fit (adjusted R², which penalizes adding useless variables) climb.

train <- dat |> filter(yearID <= TRAIN_END)
test  <- dat |> filter(yearID >  TRAIN_END)

f1 <- ops_target ~ marcel_ops                                   # recent form
f2 <- ops_target ~ marcel_ops + age + age_sq                    # + aging
f3 <- ops_target ~ marcel_ops + age + age_sq +
        lag1_Kpct + lag1_BABIP + park_bpf + lag1_PA + bats       # + box-score context
f4 <- ops_target ~ marcel_ops + age + age_sq +
        lag1_Kpct + lag1_BABIP + park_bpf + lag1_PA + bats +
        lag1_sc_xwoba                                            # + Statcast

m1 <- lm(f1, train); m2 <- lm(f2, train)
m3 <- lm(f3, train); m4 <- lm(f4, train)

step_r2 <- tibble(
  step = factor(c("Recent form", "+ Aging", "+ Box-score context", "+ Statcast (xwOBA)"),
                levels = c("Recent form", "+ Aging", "+ Box-score context", "+ Statcast (xwOBA)")),
  adj_r2 = c(summary(m1)$adj.r.squared, summary(m2)$adj.r.squared,
             summary(m3)$adj.r.squared, summary(m4)$adj.r.squared))

ggplot(step_r2, aes(step, adj_r2)) +
  geom_col(fill = col_a, width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", adj_r2)), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.4) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +
  labs(title = "How much each layer adds (training adjusted R²)",
       subtitle = "Statcast's xwOBA earns its keep on top of everything traditional",
       x = NULL, y = "Adjusted R²")

The jump from the third bar to the fourth is the payoff: adding last year’s xwOBA lifts the fit even after the model already knows recent results, age, and discipline. Expected contact quality carries signal the box score misses.

What the model says

Standardizing the inputs puts every coefficient on the same footing; each bar is the OPS change from a one-standard-deviation move in that predictor, holding the rest fixed.

num_p <- c("marcel_ops","age","age_sq","lag1_Kpct","lag1_BABIP","park_bpf",
           "lag1_PA","lag1_sc_xwoba")
train_std <- train |> mutate(across(all_of(num_p), ~ as.numeric(scale(.))))
m4_std <- lm(f4, train_std)

broom::tidy(m4_std) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(lo = estimate - 1.96*std.error, hi = estimate + 1.96*std.error) |>
  ggplot(aes(estimate, reorder(term, estimate))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "grey60") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = lo, xend = hi, yend = reorder(term, estimate)), colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(colour = col_a, size = 2.4) +
  labs(title = "What moves a hitter's projected OPS",
       subtitle = "Standardized OLS coefficients (95% CI); effect of a 1-SD change",
       x = "Change in projected OPS (per SD)", y = NULL)

Recent form (the Marcel blend) does most of the work, as it should. But prior xwOBA stands on its own as a positive, independent contributor — the model’s way of saying a hitter who earned better results than he got is a good bet to improve. A high strikeout rate drags the projection down; a high prior BABIP nudges it down too, the regression-to-the-mean instinct made literal (lucky balls-in-play don’t repeat). These predictors are correlated with one another, so read each as a partial effect; the cleaner evidence that Statcast adds value is the adjusted-R² jump above, not any single coefficient.

The aging curve, recovered

Sweeping age through the model while holding everything else at its average traces out the classic shape — a rise into the late 20s, then decline.

agrid <- 22:40
nd <- tibble(age = agrid, age_sq = agrid^2,
             marcel_ops = mean(train$marcel_ops),
             lag1_Kpct = mean(train$lag1_Kpct), lag1_BABIP = mean(train$lag1_BABIP),
             park_bpf = mean(train$park_bpf), lag1_PA = mean(train$lag1_PA),
             lag1_sc_xwoba = mean(train$lag1_sc_xwoba),
             bats = factor("R", levels = levels(train$bats)))
nd$proj <- predict(m4, newdata = nd)
peak <- nd$age[which.max(nd$proj)]

ggplot(nd, aes(age, proj)) +
  geom_line(colour = col_a, linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = peak, linetype = "dashed", colour = col_b) +
  annotate("text", x = peak + 0.3, y = min(nd$proj), hjust = 0, colour = col_b,
           size = 3.4, label = sprintf("peak ~ age %d", peak)) +
  labs(title = "The aging curve the regression learned",
       subtitle = "Projected OPS by age, holding all else constant",
       x = "Age", y = "Projected OPS")

Does it actually work?

Numbers explained in-sample mean little; the test is unseen seasons. Trained through 2022 and checked on 2023–2025, the regression is compared to two honest baselines.

rmse <- function(a, p) sqrt(mean((a - p)^2))
r2   <- function(a, p) 1 - sum((a - p)^2) / sum((a - mean(a))^2)
yte  <- test$ops_target
pred_reg <- predict(m4, newdata = test)

scores <- tibble(
  Approach = c("Persistence (last yr OPS)", "Marcel blend", "Regression + Statcast"),
  RMSE = c(rmse(yte, test$lag1_OPS), rmse(yte, test$marcel_ops), rmse(yte, pred_reg)),
  R2   = c(r2(yte, test$lag1_OPS),  r2(yte, test$marcel_ops),  r2(yte, pred_reg)))
scores
ggplot(tibble(actual = yte, pred = pred_reg), aes(actual, pred)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = col_b, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, colour = col_a) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 1.15, hjust = 0, size = 3.6,
           label = sprintf("RMSE %.3f   R² %.2f",
                           rmse(yte, pred_reg), r2(yte, pred_reg))) +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(0.45, 1.2), ylim = c(0.45, 1.2)) +
  labs(title = "Projected vs. actual next-season OPS (held-out seasons)",
       subtitle = "Dashed line = a perfect projection",
       x = "Actual OPS", y = "Projected OPS")

What it means

  • It beats the naive guesses, modestly. Predicting “same as last year” is actively worse than the model, because it re-bets last season’s luck. The regression’s edge is mostly that it pulls extremes back toward the middle.
  • It can’t see breakouts coming, by design. The model rarely projects a career year — regressing toward the mean is exactly what lowers average error. When a hitter posts a monster season, the projection will sit below it. That’s a feature of forecasting, not a flaw to fix.
  • Statcast adds a real, measured edge. Prior-year xwOBA improves the fit on top of everything the box score already says. Quality of contact is partly skill that the raw line undersells.
  • The ceiling is low because the problem is hard. Injuries, role changes, and balls-in-play variance cap how well anyone projects a single season. An R² near 0.3 is an honest model in a game with so many moving variables.

Data: Lahman database (traditional stats, bio, park factors) and Baseball Savant (expected stats), linked via the Chadwick register. Code folded throughout — click “Code” to expand any section.
